src_get_gpc.description: Same as "table_gpc" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_get_gpc.name: src_get_gpc

src_get_gpc0.description: Same as "table_gpc0" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_get_gpc0.name: src_get_gpc0

src_get_gpc1.description: Same as "table_gpc1" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_get_gpc1.name: src_get_gpc1

src_get_gpt.description: Same as "table_gpt" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_get_gpt.name: src_get_gpt

src_get_gpt0.description: Same as "table_gpt0" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_get_gpt0.name: src_get_gpt0

src_glitch_cnt.description: Same as "table_glitch_cnt" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_glitch_cnt.name: src_glitch_cnt

src_glitch_rate.description: Same as "table_glitch_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_glitch_rate.name: src_glitch_rate

src_gpc0_rate.description: Same as "table_gpc0_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_gpc0_rate.name: src_gpc0_rate

src_gpc1_rate.description: Same as "table_gpc1_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_gpc1_rate.name: src_gpc1_rate

src_gpc_rate.description: Same as "table_gpc_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_gpc_rate.name: src_gpc_rate

src_http_err_cnt.description: Same as "table_http_err_cnt" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_http_err_cnt.name: src_http_err_cnt

src_http_err_rate.description: Same as "table_http_err_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_http_err_rate.name: src_http_err_rate

src_http_fail_cnt.description: Same as "table_http_fail_cnt" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_http_fail_cnt.name: src_http_fail_cnt

src_http_fail_rate.description: Same as "table_http_fail_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_http_fail_rate.name: src_http_fail_rate

src_http_req_cnt.description: Same as "table_http_req_cnt" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_http_req_cnt.name: src_http_req_cnt

src_http_req_rate.description: Same as "table_http_req_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_http_req_rate.name: src_http_req_rate

src_inc_gpc.description: Same as "src_inc_gpc" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_inc_gpc.name: src_inc_gpc

src_inc_gpc0.description: Same as "src_inc_gpc0" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_inc_gpc0.name: src_inc_gpc0

src_inc_gpc1.description: Same as "src_inc_gpc1" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_inc_gpc1.name: src_inc_gpc1

src_is_local.description: Returns true if the source address of the incoming connection is local to the system, or false if the address doesn't exist on the system, meaning that it comes from a remote machine. Note that UNIX addresses are considered local. It can be useful to apply certain access restrictions based on where the client comes from (e.g. require auth or https for remote machines). Please note that the check involves a few system calls, so it's better to do it only once per connection.
src_is_local.name: src_is_local

src_kbytes_in.description: Same as "table_kbytes_in" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_kbytes_in.name: src_kbytes_in

src_kbytes_out.description: Same as "table_kbytes_out" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_kbytes_out.name: src_kbytes_out

src_port.description: Returns an integer value corresponding to the TCP source port of the connection on the client side, which is the port the client connected from. Any tcp/http rules may alter this address. Usage of this function is very limited as modern protocols do not care much about source ports nowadays.
src_port.name: src_port

src_sess_cnt.description: Same as "table_sess_cnt" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_sess_cnt.name: src_sess_cnt

src_sess_rate.description: Same as "table_sess_rate" converter with key set to the incoming connection's source address.
src_sess_rate.name: src_sess_rate

src_updt_conn_cnt.description: Creates or updates the entry associated to the incoming connection's source address in the current proxy's stick-table or in the designated stick-table. This table must be configured to store the "conn_cnt" data type, otherwise the match will be ignored. The current count is incremented by one, and the expiration timer refreshed. The updated count is returned, so this match can't return zero. This was used to reject service abusers based on their source address. Note: it is recommended to use the more complete "track-sc*" actions in "tcp-request" rules instead.
src_updt_conn_cnt.name: src_updt_conn_cnt

srv_conn.description: Returns an integer value corresponding to the number of currently established connections on the designated server, possibly including the connection being evaluated. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. It can be used to use a specific farm when one server is full, or to inform the server about our view of the number of active connections with it. See also the "fe_conn", "be_conn", "queue", and "srv_conn_free" fetch methods.
srv_conn.name: srv_conn

srv_conn_free.description: Returns an integer value corresponding to the number of available connections on the designated server, possibly including the connection being evaluated. The value does not include queue slots. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. It can be used to use a specific farm when one server is full, or to inform the server about our view of the number of active connections with it. See also the "be_conn_free" and "srv_conn" fetch methods.
srv_conn_free.name: srv_conn_free

srv_id.description: Returns an integer containing the server's id when processing the response. While it's almost only used with ACLs, it may be used for logging or debugging. It can also be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
srv_id.name: srv_id

srv_is_up.description: Returns true when the designated server is UP, and false when it is either DOWN or in maintenance mode. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. It is mainly used to take action based on an external status reported via a health check (e.g. a geographical site's availability). Another possible use which is more of a hack consists in using dummy servers as boolean variables that can be enabled or disabled from the CLI, so that rules depending on those ACLs can be tweaked in realtime.
srv_is_up.name: srv_is_up

srv_iweight.description: Returns an integer corresponding to the server's initial weight. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. See also "srv_weight" and "srv_uweight".
srv_iweight.name: srv_iweight

srv_name.description: Returns a string containing the server's name when processing the response. While it's almost only used with ACLs, it may be used for logging or debugging. It can also be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
srv_name.name: srv_name

srv_queue.description: Returns an integer value corresponding to the number of connections currently pending in the designated server's queue. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. It can sometimes be used together with the "use-server" directive to force to use a known faster server when it is not much loaded. See also the "srv_conn", "avg_queue" and "queue" sample fetch methods.
srv_queue.name: srv_queue

srv_sess_rate.description: Returns an integer corresponding to the sessions creation rate on the designated server, in number of new sessions per second. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. This is mostly used with ACLs but can make sense with logs too. This is used to switch to an alternate backend when an expensive or fragile one reaches too high a session rate, or to limit abuse of service (e.g. prevent latent requests from overloading servers).
srv_sess_rate.name: srv_sess_rate

srv_uweight.description: Returns an integer corresponding to the user visible server's weight. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. See also "srv_weight" and "srv_iweight".
srv_uweight.name: srv_uweight

srv_weight.description: Returns an integer corresponding to the current (or effective) server's weight. If <backend> is omitted, then the server is looked up in the current backend. See also "srv_iweight" and "srv_uweight".
srv_weight.name: srv_weight

ssl_bc.description: Returns true when the back connection was made via an SSL/TLS transport layer and is locally deciphered. This means the outgoing connection was made to a server with the "ssl" option. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc.name: ssl_bc

ssl_bc_alg_keysize.description: Returns the symmetric cipher key size supported in bits when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_alg_keysize.name: ssl_bc_alg_keysize

ssl_bc_alpn.description: This extracts the Application Layer Protocol Negotiation field from an outgoing connection made via a TLS transport layer. The result is a string containing the protocol name negotiated with the server. The SSL library must have been built with support for TLS extensions enabled (check haproxy -vv). Note that the TLS ALPN extension is not advertised unless the "alpn" keyword on the "server" line specifies a protocol list. Also, nothing forces the server to pick a protocol from this list, any other one may be requested. The TLS ALPN extension is meant to replace the TLS NPN extension. See also "ssl_bc_npn". It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_alpn.name: ssl_bc_alpn

ssl_bc_cipher.description: Returns the name of the used cipher when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_cipher.name: ssl_bc_cipher

ssl_bc_client_early_traffic_secret.description: Return the CLIENT_EARLY_TRAFFIC_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_bc_client_early_traffic_secret.name: ssl_bc_client_early_traffic_secret

ssl_bc_client_handshake_traffic_secret.description: Return the CLIENT_HANDSHAKE_TRAFFIC_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the bacl connection when the outgoing connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_bc_client_handshake_traffic_secret.name: ssl_bc_client_handshake_traffic_secret

ssl_bc_client_random.description: Returns the client random of the back connection when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to to decrypt traffic sent using ephemeral ciphers. This requires OpenSSL >= 1.1.0, or BoringSSL. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_client_random.name: ssl_bc_client_random

ssl_bc_client_traffic_secret_0.description: Return the CLIENT_TRAFFIC_SECRET_0 as an hexadecimal string for the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_bc_client_traffic_secret_0.name: ssl_bc_client_traffic_secret_0

ssl_bc_curve.description: Returns the name of the curve used in the key agreement when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. This requires OpenSSL >= 3.0.0 or AWS-LC >= 1.57.0.
ssl_bc_curve.name: ssl_bc_curve

ssl_bc_early_exporter_secret.description: Return the EARLY_EXPORTER_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over an TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_bc_early_exporter_secret.name: ssl_bc_early_exporter_secret

ssl_bc_err.description: When the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the ID of the last error of the first error stack raised on the backend side. It can raise handshake errors as well as other read or write errors occurring during the connection's lifetime. In order to get a text description of this error code, you can either use the "ssl_bc_err_str" sample fetch or use the "openssl errstr" command (which takes an error code in hexadecimal representation as parameter). Please refer to your SSL library's documentation to find the exhaustive list of error codes.
ssl_bc_err.name: ssl_bc_err

ssl_bc_err_str.description: When the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns a string representation of the last error of the first error stack that was raised on the connection from the backend's perspective. See also "ssl_fc_err".
ssl_bc_err_str.name: ssl_bc_err_str

ssl_bc_exporter_secret.description: Return the EXPORTER_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_bc_exporter_secret.name: ssl_bc_exporter_secret

ssl_bc_is_resumed.description: Returns true when the back connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer and the newly created SSL session was resumed using a cached session or a TLS ticket. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_is_resumed.name: ssl_bc_is_resumed

ssl_bc_npn.description: This extracts the Next Protocol Negotiation field from an outgoing connection made via a TLS transport layer. The result is a string containing the protocol name negotiated with the server . The SSL library must have been built with support for TLS extensions enabled (check haproxy -vv). Note that the TLS NPN extension is not advertised unless the "npn" keyword on the "server" line specifies a protocol list. Also, nothing forces the server to pick a protocol from this list, any other one may be used. Please note that the TLS NPN extension was replaced with ALPN. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_npn.name: ssl_bc_npn

ssl_bc_protocol.description: Returns the name of the used protocol when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_protocol.name: ssl_bc_protocol

ssl_bc_server_handshake_traffic_secret.description: Return the SERVER_HANDSHAKE_TRAFFIC_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_bc_server_handshake_traffic_secret.name: ssl_bc_server_handshake_traffic_secret

ssl_bc_server_random.description: Returns the server random of the back connection when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to to decrypt traffic sent using ephemeral ciphers. This requires OpenSSL >= 1.1.0, or BoringSSL. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_server_random.name: ssl_bc_server_random

ssl_bc_server_traffic_secret_0.description: Return the SERVER_TRAFFIC_SECRET_0 as an hexadecimal string for the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over an TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_bc_server_traffic_secret_0.name: ssl_bc_server_traffic_secret_0

ssl_bc_session_id.description: Returns the SSL ID of the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to log if we want to know if session was reused or not. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_session_id.name: ssl_bc_session_id

ssl_bc_session_key.description: Returns the SSL session master key of the back connection when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to decrypt traffic sent using ephemeral ciphers. This requires OpenSSL >= 1.1.0, or BoringSSL. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_session_key.name: ssl_bc_session_key

ssl_bc_sni.description: This retrieves the Server Name Indication TLS extension (SNI) field that was used on the connection to the server. The result (when present) typically is a string matching the HTTPS host name (253 chars or less). The main use case is for logging and debugging purposes (e.g. figure what SNI was used when the connection was established to match it against what the server has seen).
ssl_bc_sni.name: ssl_bc_sni

ssl_bc_unique_id.description: When the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the TLS unique ID as defined in RFC5929 section 3. The unique id can be encoded to base64 using the converter: "ssl_bc_unique_id,base64". It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_unique_id.name: ssl_bc_unique_id

ssl_bc_use_keysize.description: Returns the symmetric cipher key size used in bits when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It can be used in a tcp-check or an http-check ruleset.
ssl_bc_use_keysize.name: ssl_bc_use_keysize

ssl_c_ca_err.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the ID of the first error detected during verification of the client certificate at depth > 0, or 0 if no error was encountered during this verification process. Please refer to your SSL library's documentation to find the exhaustive list of error codes.
ssl_c_ca_err.name: ssl_c_ca_err

ssl_c_ca_err_depth.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the depth in the CA chain of the first error detected during the verification of the client certificate. If no error is encountered, 0 is returned.
ssl_c_ca_err_depth.name: ssl_c_ca_err_depth

ssl_c_chain_der.description: Returns the DER formatted chain certificate presented by the client when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form. One can parse the result with any lib accepting ASN.1 DER data. It currently does not support resumed sessions.
ssl_c_chain_der.name: ssl_c_chain_der

ssl_c_der.description: Returns the DER formatted certificate presented by the client when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form.
ssl_c_der.name: ssl_c_der

ssl_c_err.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the ID of the first error detected during verification at depth 0, or 0 if no error was encountered during this verification process. Please refer to your SSL library's documentation to find the exhaustive list of error codes.
ssl_c_err.name: ssl_c_err

ssl_c_i_dn.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the full distinguished name of the issuer of the certificate presented by the client when no <entry> is specified, or the value of the first given entry found from the beginning of the DN. If a positive/negative occurrence number is specified as the optional second argument, it returns the value of the nth given entry value from the beginning/end of the DN. For instance, "ssl_c_i_dn(OU,2)" the second organization unit, and "ssl_c_i_dn(CN)" retrieves the common name. The <format> parameter allows you to receive the DN suitable for consumption by different protocols. Currently supported is rfc2253 for LDAP v3. If you'd like to modify the format only you can specify an empty string and zero for the first two parameters. Example: ssl_c_i_dn(,0,rfc2253)
ssl_c_i_dn.name: ssl_c_i_dn

ssl_c_key_alg.description: Returns the name of the algorithm used to generate the key of the certificate presented by the client when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_c_key_alg.name: ssl_c_key_alg

ssl_c_notafter.description: Returns the end date presented by the client as a formatted string YYMMDDhhmmss[Z] when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_c_notafter.name: ssl_c_notafter

ssl_c_notbefore.description: Returns the start date presented by the client as a formatted string YYMMDDhhmmss[Z] when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_c_notbefore.name: ssl_c_notbefore

ssl_c_r_dn.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, and is successfully validated with the configured ca-file, returns the full distinguished name of the root CA of the certificate presented by the client when no <entry> is specified, or the value of the first given entry found from the beginning of the DN. If a positive/negative occurrence number is specified as the optional second argument, it returns the value of the nth given entry value from the beginning/end of the DN. For instance, "ssl_c_r_dn(OU,2)" the second organization unit, and "ssl_c_r_dn(CN)" retrieves the common name. The <format> parameter allows you to receive the DN suitable for consumption by different protocols. Currently supported is rfc2253 for LDAP v3. If you'd like to modify the format only you can specify an empty string and zero for the first two parameters. Example: ssl_c_r_dn(,0,rfc2253)
ssl_c_r_dn.name: ssl_c_r_dn

ssl_c_s_dn.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the full distinguished name of the subject of the certificate presented by the client when no <entry> is specified, or the value of the first given entry found from the beginning of the DN. If a positive/negative occurrence number is specified as the optional second argument, it returns the value of the nth given entry value from the beginning/end of the DN. For instance, "ssl_c_s_dn(OU,2)" the second organization unit, and "ssl_c_s_dn(CN)" retrieves the common name. The <format> parameter allows you to receive the DN suitable for consumption by different protocols. Currently supported is rfc2253 for LDAP v3. If you'd like to modify the format only you can specify an empty string and zero for the first two parameters. Example: ssl_c_s_dn(,0,rfc2253)
ssl_c_s_dn.name: ssl_c_s_dn

ssl_c_san.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, and was provided with a client certificate. Returns a string of comma separated Subject Alt Name fields contained into the provided certificate.
ssl_c_san.name: ssl_c_san

ssl_c_serial.description: Returns the serial of the certificate presented by the client when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form.
ssl_c_serial.name: ssl_c_serial

ssl_c_sha1.description: Returns the SHA-1 fingerprint of the certificate presented by the client when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. This can be used to stick a client to a server, or to pass this information to a server. Note that the output is binary, so if you want to pass that signature to the server, you need to encode it in hex or base64, such as in the example below:
ssl_c_sha1.name: ssl_c_sha1

ssl_c_sig_alg.description: Returns the name of the algorithm used to sign the certificate presented by the client when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_c_sig_alg.name: ssl_c_sig_alg

ssl_c_used.description: Returns true if current SSL session uses a client certificate even if current connection uses SSL session resumption. See also "ssl_fc_has_crt".
ssl_c_used.name: ssl_c_used

ssl_c_verify.description: Returns the verify result error ID when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, otherwise zero if no error is encountered. Please refer to your SSL library's documentation for an exhaustive list of error codes.
ssl_c_verify.name: ssl_c_verify

ssl_c_version.description: Returns the version of the certificate presented by the client when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_c_version.name: ssl_c_version

ssl_f_der.description: Returns the DER formatted certificate presented by the frontend when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form.
ssl_f_der.name: ssl_f_der

ssl_f_i_dn.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the full distinguished name of the issuer of the certificate presented by the frontend when no <entry> is specified, or the value of the first given entry found from the beginning of the DN. If a positive/negative occurrence number is specified as the optional second argument, it returns the value of the nth given entry value from the beginning/end of the DN. For instance, "ssl_f_i_dn(OU,2)" the second organization unit, and "ssl_f_i_dn(CN)" retrieves the common name. The <format> parameter allows you to receive the DN suitable for consumption by different protocols. Currently supported is rfc2253 for LDAP v3. If you'd like to modify the format only you can specify an empty string and zero for the first two parameters. Example: ssl_f_i_dn(,0,rfc2253)
ssl_f_i_dn.name: ssl_f_i_dn

ssl_f_key_alg.description: Returns the name of the algorithm used to generate the key of the certificate presented by the frontend when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_f_key_alg.name: ssl_f_key_alg

ssl_f_notafter.description: Returns the end date presented by the frontend as a formatted string YYMMDDhhmmss[Z] when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_f_notafter.name: ssl_f_notafter

ssl_f_notbefore.description: Returns the start date presented by the frontend as a formatted string YYMMDDhhmmss[Z] when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_f_notbefore.name: ssl_f_notbefore

ssl_f_s_dn.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the full distinguished name of the subject of the certificate presented by the frontend when no <entry> is specified, or the value of the first given entry found from the beginning of the DN. If a positive/negative occurrence number is specified as the optional second argument, it returns the value of the nth given entry value from the beginning/end of the DN. For instance, "ssl_f_s_dn(OU,2)" the second organization unit, and "ssl_f_s_dn(CN)" retrieves the common name. The <format> parameter allows you to receive the DN suitable for consumption by different protocols. Currently supported is rfc2253 for LDAP v3. If you'd like to modify the format only you can specify an empty string and zero for the first two parameters. Example: ssl_f_s_dn(,0,rfc2253)
ssl_f_s_dn.name: ssl_f_s_dn

ssl_f_serial.description: Returns the serial of the certificate presented by the frontend when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form.
ssl_f_serial.name: ssl_f_serial

ssl_f_sha1.description: Returns the SHA-1 fingerprint of the certificate presented by the frontend when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. This can be used to know which certificate was chosen using SNI.
ssl_f_sha1.name: ssl_f_sha1

ssl_f_sig_alg.description: Returns the name of the algorithm used to sign the certificate presented by the frontend when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_f_sig_alg.name: ssl_f_sig_alg

ssl_f_version.description: Returns the version of the certificate presented by the frontend when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_f_version.name: ssl_f_version

ssl_fc.description: Returns true when the front connection was made via an SSL/TLS transport layer and is locally deciphered. This means it has matched a socket declared with a "bind" line having the "ssl" option.
ssl_fc.name: ssl_fc

ssl_fc_alg_keysize.description: Returns the symmetric cipher key size supported in bits when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_fc_alg_keysize.name: ssl_fc_alg_keysize

ssl_fc_alpn.description: This extracts the Application Layer Protocol Negotiation field from an incoming connection made via a TLS transport layer and locally deciphered by HAProxy. The result is a string containing the protocol name advertised by the client. The SSL library must have been built with support for TLS extensions enabled (check haproxy -vv). Note that the TLS ALPN extension is not advertised unless the "alpn" keyword on the "bind" line specifies a protocol list. Also, nothing forces the client to pick a protocol from this list, any other one may be requested. The TLS ALPN extension is meant to replace the TLS NPN extension. See also "ssl_fc_npn".
ssl_fc_alpn.name: ssl_fc_alpn

ssl_fc_cipher.description: Returns the name of the used cipher when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_fc_cipher.name: ssl_fc_cipher

ssl_fc_cipherlist_bin.description: Returns the binary form of the client hello cipher list. The maximum returned value length is limited by the shared capture buffer size controlled by "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" setting. Setting <filter_option> allows to filter returned data. Accepted values: 0 : return the full list of ciphers (default) 1 : exclude GREASE (RFC8701) values from the output
ssl_fc_cipherlist_bin.name: ssl_fc_cipherlist_bin

ssl_fc_cipherlist_hex.description: Returns the binary form of the client hello cipher list encoded as hexadecimal. The maximum returned value length is limited by the shared capture buffer size controlled by "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" setting. Setting <filter_option> allows to filter returned data. Accepted values: 0 : return the full list of ciphers (default) 1 : exclude GREASE (RFC8701) values from the output
ssl_fc_cipherlist_hex.name: ssl_fc_cipherlist_hex

ssl_fc_cipherlist_str.description: Returns the decoded text form of the client hello cipher list. The maximum returned value length is limited by the shared capture buffer size controlled by "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" setting. Setting <filter_option> allows to filter returned data. Accepted values: 0 : return the full list of ciphers (default) 1 : exclude GREASE (RFC8701) values from the output Note that this sample-fetch is only available with OpenSSL >= 1.0.2. If the function is not enabled, this sample-fetch returns the hash like "ssl_fc_cipherlist_xxh".
ssl_fc_cipherlist_str.name: ssl_fc_cipherlist_str

ssl_fc_cipherlist_xxh.description: Returns a xxh64 of the cipher list. This hash can return only if the value "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" is set greater than 0, however the hash take into account all the data of the cipher list.
ssl_fc_cipherlist_xxh.name: ssl_fc_cipherlist_xxh

ssl_fc_client_early_traffic_secret.description: Return the CLIENT_EARLY_TRAFFIC_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the front connection when the incoming connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_fc_client_early_traffic_secret.name: ssl_fc_client_early_traffic_secret

ssl_fc_client_handshake_traffic_secret.description: Return the CLIENT_HANDSHAKE_TRAFFIC_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the front connection when the incoming connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_fc_client_handshake_traffic_secret.name: ssl_fc_client_handshake_traffic_secret

ssl_fc_client_random.description: Returns the client random of the front connection when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to to decrypt traffic sent using ephemeral ciphers. This requires OpenSSL >= 1.1.0, or BoringSSL.
ssl_fc_client_random.name: ssl_fc_client_random

ssl_fc_client_traffic_secret_0.description: Return the CLIENT_TRAFFIC_SECRET_0 as an hexadecimal string for the front connection when the incoming connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_fc_client_traffic_secret_0.name: ssl_fc_client_traffic_secret_0

ssl_fc_curve.description: Returns the name of the curve used in the key agreement when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. This requires OpenSSL >= 3.0.0.
ssl_fc_curve.name: ssl_fc_curve

ssl_fc_early_exporter_secret.description: Return the EARLY_EXPORTER_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the front connection when the incoming connection was made over an TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_fc_early_exporter_secret.name: ssl_fc_early_exporter_secret

ssl_fc_early_rcvd.description: Returns true if early data were seen over that connection, regardless of the fact that the handshake has since completed. It has no practical use case for traffic processing, however it's about the only way to "see" that a client used 0-RTT to send early data, and is sometimes useful when debugging, since the only other alternatives are network traffic captures or logging the front connection's flags and matching them in the code. It may also be useful to get statistics on clients' capabilities. See also "ssl_fc_has_early".
ssl_fc_early_rcvd.name: ssl_fc_early_rcvd

ssl_fc_ecformats_bin.description: Return the binary form of the client hello supported elliptic curve point formats. The maximum returned value length is limited by the shared capture buffer size controlled by "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" setting.
ssl_fc_ecformats_bin.name: ssl_fc_ecformats_bin

ssl_fc_eclist_bin.description: Returns the binary form of the client hello supported elliptic curves. The maximum returned value length is limited by the shared capture buffer size controlled by "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" setting. Setting <filter_option> allows to filter returned data. Accepted values: 0 : return the full list of supported elliptic curves (default) 1 : exclude GREASE (RFC8701) values from the output
ssl_fc_eclist_bin.name: ssl_fc_eclist_bin

ssl_fc_err.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the ID of the last error of the first error stack raised on the frontend side, or 0 if no error was encountered. It can be used to identify handshake related errors other than verify ones (such as cipher mismatch), as well as other read or write errors occurring during the connection's lifetime.  Any error happening during the client's certificate verification process will not be raised through this fetch but via the existing "ssl_c_err", "ssl_c_ca_err" and "ssl_c_ca_err_depth" fetches. In order to get a text description of this error code, you can either use the "ssl_fc_err_str" sample fetch or use the "openssl errstr" command (which takes an error code in hexadecimal representation as parameter). Please refer to your SSL library's documentation to find the exhaustive list of error codes.
ssl_fc_err.name: ssl_fc_err

ssl_fc_err_str.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns a string representation of the last error of the first error stack that was raised on the frontend side. Any error happening during the client's certificate verification process will not be raised through this fetch. See also "ssl_fc_err".
ssl_fc_err_str.name: ssl_fc_err_str

ssl_fc_exporter_secret.description: Return the EXPORTER_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the front connection when the incoming connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_fc_exporter_secret.name: ssl_fc_exporter_secret

ssl_fc_extlist_bin.description: Returns the binary form of the client hello extension list. The maximum returned value length is limited by the shared capture buffer size controlled by "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" setting. Setting <filter_option> allows to filter returned data. Accepted values: 0 : return the full list of extensions (default) 1 : exclude GREASE (RFC8701) values from the output
ssl_fc_extlist_bin.name: ssl_fc_extlist_bin

ssl_fc_has_crt.description: Returns true if a client certificate is present in an incoming connection over SSL/TLS transport layer. Useful if 'verify' statement is set to 'optional'.
ssl_fc_has_crt.name: ssl_fc_has_crt

ssl_fc_has_early.description: Returns true if early data were sent, and the handshake didn't complete yet. As it has security implications, it is useful to be able to refuse those, or wait until the handshake completes (via the "wait-for-handshake" action). See also "ssl_fc_early_rcvd".
ssl_fc_has_early.name: ssl_fc_has_early

ssl_fc_has_sni.description: This checks for the presence of a Server Name Indication TLS extension (SNI) in an incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. Returns true when the incoming connection presents a TLS SNI field. This requires that the SSL library is built with support for TLS extensions enabled (check haproxy -vv).
ssl_fc_has_sni.name: ssl_fc_has_sni

ssl_fc_is_resumed.description: Returns true if the SSL/TLS session has been resumed through the use of SSL session cache or TLS tickets on an incoming connection over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_fc_is_resumed.name: ssl_fc_is_resumed

ssl_fc_npn.description: This extracts the Next Protocol Negotiation field from an incoming connection made via a TLS transport layer and locally deciphered by HAProxy. The result is a string containing the protocol name advertised by the client. The SSL library must have been built with support for TLS extensions enabled (check haproxy -vv). Note that the TLS NPN extension is not advertised unless the "npn" keyword on the "bind" line specifies a protocol list. Also, nothing forces the client to pick a protocol from this list, any other one may be requested. Please note that the TLS NPN extension was replaced with ALPN.
ssl_fc_npn.name: ssl_fc_npn

ssl_fc_protocol.description: Returns the name of the used protocol when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_fc_protocol.name: ssl_fc_protocol

ssl_fc_protocol_hello_id.description: The version of the TLS protocol by which the client wishes to communicate during the session as indicated in client hello message. This value can return only if the value "tune.ssl.capture-buffer-size" is set greater than 0.
ssl_fc_protocol_hello_id.name: ssl_fc_protocol_hello_id

ssl_fc_server_handshake_traffic_secret.description: Return the SERVER_HANDSHAKE_TRAFFIC_SECRET as an hexadecimal string for the front connection when the incoming connection was made over a TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_fc_server_handshake_traffic_secret.name: ssl_fc_server_handshake_traffic_secret

ssl_fc_server_random.description: Returns the server random of the front connection when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to to decrypt traffic sent using ephemeral ciphers. This requires OpenSSL >= 1.1.0, or BoringSSL.
ssl_fc_server_random.name: ssl_fc_server_random

ssl_fc_server_traffic_secret_0.description: Return the SERVER_TRAFFIC_SECRET_0 as an hexadecimal string for the front connection when the incoming connection was made over an TLS 1.3 transport layer. Require OpenSSL >= 1.1.1. This is one of the keys dumped by the OpenSSL keylog callback to generate the SSLKEYLOGFILE. The SSL Key logging must be activated with "tune.ssl.keylog on" in the global section. See also "tune.ssl.keylog"
ssl_fc_server_traffic_secret_0.name: ssl_fc_server_traffic_secret_0

ssl_fc_session_id.description: Returns the SSL ID of the front connection when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to stick a given client to a server. It is important to note that some browsers refresh their session ID every few minutes.
ssl_fc_session_id.name: ssl_fc_session_id

ssl_fc_session_key.description: Returns the SSL session master key of the front connection when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. It is useful to decrypt traffic sent using ephemeral ciphers. This requires OpenSSL >= 1.1.0, or BoringSSL.
ssl_fc_session_key.name: ssl_fc_session_key

ssl_fc_sigalgs_bin.description: Returns the content of the signatures_algorithms (13) TLS extension presented during the Client Hello. It provides a binary list of 2-bytes algorithms defined in the TLS RFC: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8446#section-4.2.3.
ssl_fc_sigalgs_bin.name: ssl_fc_sigalgs_bin

ssl_fc_sni.description: This extracts the Server Name Indication TLS extension (SNI) field from an incoming connection made via an SSL/TLS transport layer and locally deciphered by HAProxy. The result (when present) typically is a string matching the HTTPS host name (253 chars or less). The SSL library must have been built with support for TLS extensions enabled (check haproxy -vv).
ssl_fc_sni.name: ssl_fc_sni

ssl_fc_supported_versions_bin.description: Returns the content of the supported_versions (43) TLS extension presented during the Client Hello. It provides a binary list of 2-bytes versions. TLSv1.3 (0x0304), TLSv1.2 (0x0303).
ssl_fc_supported_versions_bin.name: ssl_fc_supported_versions_bin

ssl_fc_unique_id.description: When the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the TLS unique ID as defined in RFC5929 section 3. The unique id can be encoded to base64 using the converter: "ssl_fc_unique_id,base64".
ssl_fc_unique_id.name: ssl_fc_unique_id

ssl_fc_use_keysize.description: Returns the symmetric cipher key size used in bits when the incoming connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_fc_use_keysize.name: ssl_fc_use_keysize

ssl_s_chain_der.description: Returns the DER formatted chain certificate presented by the server when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form. One can parse the result with any lib accepting ASN.1 DER data. It currently does not support resumed sessions.
ssl_s_chain_der.name: ssl_s_chain_der

ssl_s_der.description: Returns the DER formatted certificate presented by the server when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form.
ssl_s_der.name: ssl_s_der

ssl_s_i_dn.description: When the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the full distinguished name of the issuer of the certificate presented by the server when no <entry> is specified, or the value of the first given entry found from the beginning of the DN. If a positive/negative occurrence number is specified as the optional second argument, it returns the value of the nth given entry value from the beginning/end of the DN. For instance, "ssl_s_i_dn(OU,2)" the second organization unit, and "ssl_s_i_dn(CN)" retrieves the common name. The <format> parameter allows you to receive the DN suitable for consumption by different protocols. Currently supported is rfc2253 for LDAP v3. If you'd like to modify the format only you can specify an empty string and zero for the first two parameters. Example: ssl_s_i_dn(,0,rfc2253)
ssl_s_i_dn.name: ssl_s_i_dn

ssl_s_key_alg.description: Returns the name of the algorithm used to generate the key of the certificate presented by the server when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_s_key_alg.name: ssl_s_key_alg

ssl_s_notafter.description: Returns the end date presented by the server as a formatted string YYMMDDhhmmss[Z] when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_s_notafter.name: ssl_s_notafter

ssl_s_notbefore.description: Returns the start date presented by the server as a formatted string YYMMDDhhmmss[Z] when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_s_notbefore.name: ssl_s_notbefore

ssl_s_s_dn.description: When the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer, returns the full distinguished name of the subject of the certificate presented by the server when no <entry> is specified, or the value of the first given entry found from the beginning of the DN. If a positive/negative occurrence number is specified as the optional second argument, it returns the value of the nth given entry value from the beginning/end of the DN. For instance, "ssl_s_s_dn(OU,2)" the second organization unit, and "ssl_s_s_dn(CN)" retrieves the common name. The <format> parameter allows you to receive the DN suitable for consumption by different protocols. Currently supported is rfc2253 for LDAP v3. If you'd like to modify the format only you can specify an empty string and zero for the first two parameters. Example: ssl_s_s_dn(,0,rfc2253)
ssl_s_s_dn.name: ssl_s_s_dn

ssl_s_serial.description: Returns the serial of the certificate presented by the server when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. When used for an ACL, the value(s) to match against can be passed in hexadecimal form.
ssl_s_serial.name: ssl_s_serial

ssl_s_sha1.description: Returns the SHA-1 fingerprint of the certificate presented by the server when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer. This can be used to know which certificate was chosen using SNI.
ssl_s_sha1.name: ssl_s_sha1

ssl_s_sig_alg.description: Returns the name of the algorithm used to sign the certificate presented by the server when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_s_sig_alg.name: ssl_s_sig_alg

ssl_s_version.description: Returns the version of the certificate presented by the server when the outgoing connection was made over an SSL/TLS transport layer.
ssl_s_version.name: ssl_s_version

status.description: Returns an integer containing the HTTP status code in the HTTP response, for example, 302. It is mostly used within ACLs and integer ranges, for example, to remove any Location header if the response is not a 3xx. It will be the status code received by the client if it is not changed, via a 'set-status' action for instance.
status.name: status

stopping.description: Returns TRUE if the process calling the function is currently stopping. This can be useful for logging, or for relaxing certain checks or helping close certain connections upon graceful shutdown.
stopping.name: stopping

table_avl.description: Returns the total number of available entries in the current proxy's stick-table or in the designated stick-table. See also "table_cnt".
table_avl.name: table_avl

table_cnt.description: Returns the total number of entries currently in use in the current proxy's stick-table or in the designated stick-table. See also "table_conn_cnt" and table_avl for other entry counting methods.
table_cnt.name: table_cnt

term_events.description: Returns all known termination events for all entities attached a stream, on client and server sides. A tuple of seven elements is returned with following info:
term_events.name: term_events

thread.description: Returns an integer value corresponding to the position of the thread calling the function, between 0 and (global.nbthread-1). This is useful for logging and debugging purposes.
thread.name: thread

txn.conn_retries.description: Returns the the number of connection retries experienced by this stream when trying to connect to the server. This value is subject to change while the connection is not fully established. For HTTP connections, the value may be affected by L7 retries.
txn.conn_retries.name: txn.conn_retries

txn.id32.description: Returns the internal transaction ID. It is a 32bits integer. So, in absolute, its value is not unique, transaction IDs may wrap. The wrapping period depends on the request rate. In practice, it should not be an issue. For a true unique ID, see "unique-id-format" directive.
txn.id32.name: txn.id32

txn.redispatched.description: Returns true if the connection has experienced redispatch upon retry according to "option redispatch" configuration. This value is subject to change while the connection is not fully established. For HTTP connections, the value may be affected by L7 retries.
txn.redispatched.name: txn.redispatched

txn.sess_term_state.description: Returns the TCP or HTTP stream termination state, as reported in the log. It is a 2-characters string, The final stream state followed by the event which caused its to terminate. See section 8.5 about stream state at disconnection for the list of possible events. The current value at time the sample fetch is evaluated is returned. It is subject to change. Except used with ACLs in "http-after-response" rule sets or in log messages, it will always be "--".
txn.sess_term_state.name: txn.sess_term_state

txn.status.description: Return an integer containing the HTTP status code of the transaction, as reported in the log.
txn.status.name: txn.status

txn.timer.total.description: Total active time for the HTTP request, between the moment the proxy received the first byte of the request header and the emission of the last byte of the response body. This is the equivalent of %Ta in the log-format and is reported in milliseconds (ms).  For more information see Section 8.4 "Timing events"
txn.timer.total.name: txn.timer.total

txn.timer.user.description: Total estimated time as seen from client, between the moment the proxy accepted it and the moment both ends were closed, without idle time. This is the equivalent of %Tu in the log-format and is reported in milliseconds (ms). For more details see Section 8.4 "Timing events"
txn.timer.user.name: txn.timer.user

unique-id.description: Returns the unique-id attached to the request. The directive "unique-id-format" must be set. If it is not set, the unique-id sample fetch fails. Note that the unique-id is usually used with HTTP requests, however this sample fetch can be used with other protocols. Obviously, if it is used with other protocols than HTTP, the unique-id-format directive must not contain HTTP parts. See: unique-id-format and unique-id-header
unique-id.name: unique-id

uptime.description: Returns the uptime of the current HAProxy worker in seconds.
uptime.name: uptime

url.description: This extracts the request's URL as presented in the request. A typical use is with prefetch-capable caches, and with portals which need to aggregate multiple information from databases and keep them in caches. With ACLs, using "path" is preferred over using "url", because clients may send a full URL as is normally done with proxies. The only real use is to match "*" which does not match in "path", and for which there is already a predefined ACL. See also "path" and "base". Please note that any fragment reference in the URI ('#' after the path) is strictly forbidden by the HTTP standard and will be rejected. However, if the frontend receiving the request has "option accept-unsafe-violations-in-http-request", then this fragment part will be accepted and will also appear in the url.
url.name: url

url32.description: This returns a 32-bit hash of the value obtained by concatenating the first Host header and the whole URL including parameters (not only the path part of the request, as in the "base32" fetch above). This is useful to track per-URL activity. A shorter hash is stored, saving a lot of memory. The output type is an unsigned integer.
url32.name: url32

url_ip.description: This extracts the IP address from the request's URL when the host part is presented as an IP address. Its use is very limited. For instance, a monitoring system might use this field as an alternative for the source IP in order to test what path a given source address would follow, or to force an entry in a table for a given source address. It may be used in combination with 'http-request set-dst' to emulate the older 'option http_proxy'.
url_ip.name: url_ip

url_param.description: This extracts the first occurrence of the parameter <name> in the query string, which begins after either '?' or <delim>, and which ends before '&', ';' or <delim>. The parameter name is case-sensitive, unless"i" is added as a third argument. If no name is given, any parameter will match, and the first one will be returned. The result is a string corresponding to the value of the parameter <name> as presented in the request (no URL decoding is performed). This can be used for session stickiness based on a client ID, to extract an application cookie passed as a URL parameter, or in ACLs to apply some checks. Note that the ACL version of this fetch iterates over multiple parameters and will iteratively report all parameters values if no name is given
url_param.name: url_param

url_port.description: This extracts the port part from the request's URL. Note that if the port is not specified in the request, port 80 is assumed..
url_port.name: url_port

urlp.description: This extracts the first occurrence of the parameter <name> in the query string, which begins after either '?' or <delim>, and which ends before '&', ';' or <delim>. The parameter name is case-sensitive, unless"i" is added as a third argument. If no name is given, any parameter will match, and the first one will be returned. The result is a string corresponding to the value of the parameter <name> as presented in the request (no URL decoding is performed). This can be used for session stickiness based on a client ID, to extract an application cookie passed as a URL parameter, or in ACLs to apply some checks. Note that the ACL version of this fetch iterates over multiple parameters and will iteratively report all parameters values if no name is given
urlp.name: urlp

urlp_val.description: See "urlp" above. This one extracts the URL parameter <name> in the request and converts it to an integer value. This can be used for session stickiness based on a user ID for example, or with ACLs to match a page number or price.
urlp_val.name: urlp_val

uuid.description: Returns a UUID following the RFC 9562 standard. If the version is not specified, a UUID version 4 (fully random) is returned.
uuid.name: uuid

var.description: Returns a variable with the stored type. If the variable is not set, the sample fetch fails, unless a default value is provided, in which case it will return it as a string. Empty strings are permitted. See section 2.8 about variables for details.
var.name: var

wait_end.description: This fetch either returns true when the inspection period is over, or does not fetch. It is only used in ACLs, in conjunction with content analysis to avoid returning a wrong verdict early. It may also be used to delay some actions, such as a delayed reject for some special addresses. Since it either stops the rules evaluation or immediately returns true, it is recommended to use this acl as the last one in a rule. Please note that the default ACL "WAIT_END" is always usable without prior declaration. This test was designed to be used with TCP request content inspection.
wait_end.name: wait_end

waiting_entity.description: This returns the identity of the entity that was waiting to continue its processing when an error or a timeout was encountered. It may be the a rule or a filter for instance. However, this list is not exhaustive and the format of all possible entities is not forcefully documented.
waiting_entity.name: waiting_entity
